title: Veterans Infertility Treatment Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.
plain_summary: This bill makes the VA provide fertility treatments to veterans who have trouble having babies and their partners. Veterans can get up to three successful rounds of in vitro fertilization or up to 10 tries, whichever happens first. The VA will also pay for travel costs when veterans or their partners need to go somewhere for treatment. Veterans can use donated eggs or sperm for their treatments if needed. The VA has one year to write the rules for how these treatments will work.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Health.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Veterans Infertility Treatment Act of two thousand and twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2  Infertility Treatments For Veterans
sections.text: The Department of Veterans Affairs must provide infertility treatments and fertility preservation services to eligible veterans and their partners. This includes treatments like in vitro fertilization where doctors help create pregnancies outside the body and then place embryos in the uterus. Veterans can receive up to three complete in vitro fertilization cycles that result in a live birth or up to ten attempts, whichever happens first. The VA can use donated sperm, eggs, or embryos for these treatments. Before any treatment begins, the VA must get written permission from the veteran, their partner if applicable, and any donor involved. The VA does not have to provide pregnancy care beyond what is already required by other laws. State laws will determine what happens to sperm, eggs, or embryos including who owns them and how they can be used or destroyed. The VA will pay travel costs for partners of veterans to receive these treatments just like they do for veterans. A covered veteran is someone enrolled in VA healthcare who either cannot have children or is at risk of losing their ability to have children due to medical treatments like chemotherapy or surgery. Infertility means either being unable to get pregnant or carry a pregnancy to birth after one year of trying without birth control. A partner is someone the veteran chooses to share parenting responsibilities with for any child born from these treatments. Standard fertility preservation services include collecting, freezing, and storing sperm, eggs, and embryos. The VA must create rules for this program within one year of this law passing. Veterans already getting fertility treatments can choose to continue with their current treatment or switch to this new program. Starting one hundred and eighty days after this law passes, the VA must expand current fertility services to include treatments for unmarried partners and the use of donated sperm, eggs, or embryos.
